speaker: interviewer
text: A little bit more in the center, just about--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the center? OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's it. OK. [INAUDIBLE]. Oops. You can sit down next to her just for the very beginning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Should I take this here? OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. He's going to use this just to identify.

speaker: interviewee
text: To identify, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I'll move over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Speed.

speaker: interviewer
text: June 23. 1995. I am Mel Kartzmer interviewing Brenda Senders in Hallandale, Florida. The interview will be in English, tape one.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You can cut. Speed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us your name, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Brenda Senders.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Poland it was at that time, [? at ?] the Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the-- What was the name of the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the town was Sarny.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sarny, S-A-R-N-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was August 20th, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name that your parents gave you at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- I remember [? Brendel. ?] At birth my first name was [? Brendel. ?] [? Bronia ?] they called me in Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was Szyr, S-Z-Y-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Your father's first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's first name was Samuel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And approximately when was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the late-- in the beginning of 1900 or 1898, I'm not-- I'm not sure of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he also born in Sarny?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born not far from Sarny. I-- as I remember it, my recollection what I was told, in [? Karminey, ?] [? Karminey ?] they called it. It's a small-- as far as I know it, [? Karminey ?] was a-- a village, or it was a small little town. I don't know. And he came from a very fine family, as I understood at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother's first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's first name was Leah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was born approximately when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately maybe a year younger than my father. I don't know, maybe the same. I-- the same, I would call it the same. Let's say in 1900.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born near Sarny, in a small-- it's like a village, [? Karpilufka ?]. I think that's what is the name, yeah, [? Karpilufka. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maiden family name was Borko.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had two sisters and a brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sisters' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister's name was Sima. The other one was Esther. And my brother's name was Solomon, [? Shalomke ?] they called him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year were your sisters and your brother born?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I-- Sima is in 1933, I think September. And my little brother was approximately 1936, sister 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your sisters and your brother survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: One sister, Sima, who was from 1933, she survived the war with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were killed, together with my little brother and my little sister, was murdered.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year-- and what year did they die?

speaker: interviewee
text: They died in 1942, [? approximately ?] in August.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone else--

speaker: interviewee
text: They murdered them, actually. They didn't die.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone else live in your home before the war, grandparents or boarders?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather was Ziskin Szyr. And he was quite a modern-- in his time was a very modern man. He used to travel a lot. And he was, at one point he was telling me there was-- he was well-to-do man. And then when the Bolshevik came, and the Communists, they took away from him. And he used be a traveler. He used to travel to Austria, he told me, and to Germany. And he met the-- the Kaiser. He used to tell me, I was fascinated with his little stories as a kid, fascinated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your grandmother was not living at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at the time of the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --your grandfather was approximately how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I can show you. I [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: No, approximately.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would call it in the 60s.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And did he also die at the same time as your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: They died the same day, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's business?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was seasonal. He was a forester, like buying of quarter of-- of woods. And then buying them off from-- from-- from-- it was owned by the government, from the [NON-ENGLISH], the-- the foresters. Buying them off and then on-- cut-- cut them down, and then in the winter, they used to come with the slate, I remember, peasants. Like 20, 50, 60. I don't know, whole bunches, like a whole-- one after the other, a train of them. They used to come, they used to load those trees. And they used to, by train, bring 'em to Sarny. And from Sarny, they used to load 'em on-- on-- on transports. And they used to take 'em to Gdynia, to Gdansk, I don't know. This is I don't know. But this is as the-- as far as I can understand. Because I was a school girl, and I was not interested in all those things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your address before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- in the beginning was Sarny. I remember we lived-- we lived on [? Olitza ?] [? Hondlova-- ?] [? Olitza ?] [? Spulna ?], I'm sorry. [? Olitza ?] [? Spulna ?], they call it. That means the street, [? Olitza ?] is in Polish, [? Olitza ?] is street, [? Spulna. ?] That's the way they called the street. I don't know the address. I don't remember the number.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live-- did you live in a house, or an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a little small house. It was a apartment, I don't know. Little homes, you know, it's a small town. So there were homes and-- and our--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you live--

speaker: interviewee
text: We consisted of three rooms, something, we had to share the kitchen or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live right in the town of Sarny? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in the town of Sarny, till my father lost-- it was a seasonal-- lost his job, and the time was very rough. And in-- in the '30s, I don't remember, '35, we moved into Rudnia, Rudnia, Rudnia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was Rudnia?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a village about 15-- about 16 kilometers from Sarny. And there my-- we opened a little little, like a grocery store. And we used to-- my mother used to bake breads and this, and we used to sell it. And that's how we made a living. And my father, had again, if he had a job, he used to accept, because it was in his field. And he was doing the buying, and-- and again, doing the same. And he knew the peasants. And they loved him, my father. They used-- they liked him. I-- I know as a fact, when I escaped from-- I escaped in the tough times. And I told them that I am [NON-ENGLISH], that means Samuel's daughter, they used to cry over me, and give me food. And he was-- he was helping people.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's go back a little bit now. Just before the war you were in school, you were a student, at my--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was a student.

speaker: interviewer
text: What school did you attend?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to a Tarbut gymnasium they call it. It's a Hebrew-- and it was acknowledged by the government, of course. And we were checked, you know. Like inspectors used to come and check out if we have the curriculum and everything is there. And it was a Hebrew gymnasium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents very Orthodox?

speaker: interviewee
text: Predominately Hebrew [? it was. ?] If they were-- if they were Orthodox, my mother was more so than my father. My father was a very modern, very updated. You know. He was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you attend shul regularly?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very modern. What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you attend shul regularly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not regularly, but to we used to go to shul, especially on the holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the shul was in Sarny?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sarny, yes. We-- I remember my father-- and I remember [NON-ENGLISH], you know. Especially when the kids used to come, I remember going with my father. And they called it this-- this-- this synagogue [NON-ENGLISH] Shul. You know, this is a commerce. All people who were in commerce or something, they belonged under or something, I don't know. But this was the shul. And I liked-- I remember liked very much to go with my father whenever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would-- would you remember the name of the rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the rabbi, no. But the name of the second shul, I remember, Rabbi [? Karlinkski, ?] I think-- Kunda, I'm sorry, Rabbi Kunda.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I went to school. And a few times I was dragged to go to-- to-- with-- with my friends to come with them, and this was like a [? Gordonia ?] or something. We used to dance and this, and horahs. And-- and that was appealing for a certain time. And then I did not proceed. I was busy with my school. Was busy with all these things, with whatever was going on. So I did not. I know from my family, a lot even went as-- as pioneers, went to Palestine at that time, so my aunt, and some of my uncles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, before the Germans came into Poland--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --how aware was your family of what was happening to the Jews in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- we are talking about the '30s, you understand. We heard-- we heard bad news. And we heard-- because it started, and it was imported, things from the Germans. Then all of a sudden, we had a Madame [? Priester ?] I remember, Madame [? Priester ?] who was the one who decided. She started brewing trouble in Poland. She was in-- like a import from the Germans. And all of a sudden, they decided, because by the Jewish law you have to slaughter a certain way a chicken and a cow and whatever. She decided it's too-- it's too painful or it's too cruel, whatever. They have to kill it by a bullet. And this is against the Jewish-- against the Jewish laws and against the Jewish rituals or whatever. And I remember the trouble start already brewing. And-- and this was now after, we understand this was imported from Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large a city was Sarny?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sarny was 20,000 population.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jews was 6,000. About 6,000, to be-- I think this is the correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel anti-Semitism in Sarny?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some-- somewhat, you know, really with our neighbors some, you know, bad and good. Some-- some weren't as-- you know, they hated. They were a hating people. So we had, you know, ups and downs with them. But we didn't feel so bad. The [INAUDIBLE], they were, you know, the students, the-- the-- the Jewish who went to our Polish school, they had-- they had a-- a worse feeling than I. Because I went to a-- to a Hebrew, where mostly predominantly Jewish kids went on there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a radio in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the end, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you getting news?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you getting news of what was happening in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the Soviets we had a radio. And-- and then, of course, in 1939, war started with us. War started with us in 1939. And then, of course, Poland was split in two. I remember the pact between the Germans and the--

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a nonaggression pact.

speaker: interviewee
text: The nonaggression pact--

speaker: interviewer
text: With Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: --was signed between the Germans and the-- and the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And by this doing they split Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which [? section-- ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I fell into the jurisdiction of the Soviets. I went to school. I proceeded still as normal. Then we start hearing right away some bad-- bad news. But it did not affected us. A lot of people started running from the other side, of the German side, wind up-- [NON-ENGLISH] we called them. This is refugees, you know, to be-- to [? translated. ?] And they start telling us horrifying story. But it did not affect us yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your family felt safe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Felt safe, I went to school. In-- and-- and we felt-- we felt all right. My father, of course, could not, that changed completely different situation was already. He-- he-- but he was working still in his same field. He was a-- a director they made. They gave him a title in there, with a lot of people under his supervision, whatever. And-- and then, of course--

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you remember the war starting between Germany and Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, I remember distinctly and very well. It was the day-- it was a warm day, a hot day, I remember. And we did not till the-- till the afternoon that the war started. Actually, they attacked the Soviets with no warning whatsoever. They attacked. And within a week, we were overrun by the Germans. And I remember we were very fearful. Because some penetrated to us news that-- and I begged, I remember, my parents. Let's run together with the Soviets. Let's retreat with them. And I remember my father said, whatever will be with [NON-ENGLISH] will be with [NON-ENGLISH]. It means whatever will be with the-- with the-- with the-- with the older Jews will be with me. And he-- he was a very-- he was a educated man. He spoke-- you know, he spoke German fluently. He was an interpreter during the First World War, as I understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was in the Army, the Polish Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the Russian, or I don't know. In the Russian, I would say, Polish and Russian, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your parents talking with their friends about those who are leaving the town to go east to Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was no time for it. It was so-- such a fast-- doing it was so fast done, it was you had to decide [BACKGROUND NOISE] within a day or two.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you remember the Germans coming into your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: How do I remember? We were in the village. And-- and some of the persons gave us hopes, don't worry. You know, stay, we will protect you. But this was just words. And then did you-- did you ask me?

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you remember the Germans coming into your town, into Sarny?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Sarny, never heard it. Because we were already still in-- in Rudnia, in-- in the village. But we knew. They didn't come exactly to Rudnia. They didn't. It was not a way for them to go through. Because it was away in the-- in the woods, and away-- it's a village.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we heard. And we heard some bad news. Some of them, they-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, some of the girls, the first line of the soldiers, came in and they-- they attacked some Jewish girls. And they-- what do they call? They raped them. They raped them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And-- and that's-- and with no time, a decree came out. They let us stay for a little while. That all the Jews must congregate, must come into Sarny, from the villages, from all the small towns, from the villages in particular, and my grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the-- how did they notify the villagers that they-- that they have to come in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through the Jewish Kehilla, through the Jewish council, through the Jewish council.

speaker: interviewer
text: So somebody came to your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Somebody came. And they were in communication with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now this was in 1942? It was in the summer of '42?

speaker: interviewee
text: --when the war started, in 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened? We moved in, in the ghetto, with-- we moved in, in the ghetto with some of our distant family, also Borko was their name. And we lived in three small rooms with about 14 people living down there, with a little kitchen that we used-- used to share.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a wall around the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was--?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there-- was there a wall around the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- we were-- not in our case. It was fenced in by a fence, a-- a wooden fence. It was not barbed wire. But it was a wooden fence. But nobody was allowed, of course, to go across the fence except through the gate. If somebody would jump through a gate, they would be killed. Because there was stationed some policemen, Ukrainians, Ukrainians and some Germans, but mostly Ukrainians, the [NON-ENGLISH], I call them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were the people given jobs to do, work to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Every family had to come to the-- to the municipality, to the self-govern, to the Kehilla, they called it, you know, to the council. They had to come, they had to produce every family has to produce two-- two or one person, almost twice a week or three times a week. And they used to send them to a manual-- and stupid work, whatever had to be done.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did the ghetto last?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto lasted from 1941 to 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Our ghetto was at one point surrounded. We heard some news penetrated in little-- little cut-outs from the newspapers, what they-- they-- for the [? populace, ?] not for the ghetto people, for the population outside. They are transferring Jews from this and this town. And then we find out that there is-- was already transferring Jews. It's a bad news. It's-- they are killing them. They are doing something with them, and we could not figure out. But we knew that something horrible things are happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next, our ghetto was surrounded one night. And what-- right away a decree, right in the [? thing, ?] a big placard, a placard came. It was written right in the-- on the front of the municipality that the-- all the [? populace ?] of the ghetto must come to the gate, to the square, where the gate was the next day. Our ghetto was surrounded with-- with Ukrainians. The-- not the-- the Ukrainians who were helping the Germans. They were Nazis just as much, and Germans. And on that day, people start-- we saw it's a panic, a panic right away. Women with children start running from street to street. And they used to-- just spiteful, they used to throw in-- used to with-- with real live ammunition, they used to throw 'em into the crowd. And some of them were killed right there and then. And my mother, right away we were-- we were a little bit away from the-- from the main crowd. My mother insisted I should go into hiding. I didn't want to. But she absolutely insisted. So I decided to go into hiding. She said, maybe-- maybe some of-- maybe one of us will survive or something, whatever. Go into hiding. And the hiding place was at my uncle's house. It was a barn for cows. It was a double wall erected. And there a little opening, you open up. You went in down there. And you close. You masqueraded. And then if a soldier or somebody would walk in, he wouldn't see nothing. Because we were-- on the-- the double wall was-- in between it in the double wall. Then I walked in there, but eight or nine people were sitting down there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this barn inside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was inside the ghetto, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But belonged to my uncle's mother-in-law or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I think you indicated that the Germans were building a camp on the outskirts of town. Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in fear every day. And one day, we heard that they took some people from our ghetto to build some kind of camp, a camp in the outskirts of our town. It was like in a-- in a little valley. It was surrounded by little hills. We were-- didn't have mountains, hills. And it was in a little valley. And they built down there a camp. Right away, we-- we were suspicious. The people who were building, asking the-- the Germans who were supervising them, what is it for. They said we are-- have to bring prisoners of war from the front in there. And that's where we are going to keep them. And we-- we didn't have no choice to believe them. And they deceived us. This was camp was erected for the people of our town, plus the people from all the small towns. And I'm speaking 6,000 Jewish inhabitants of ours, plus between 16 about 17,000 people, I can be shy a few hundred. They brought them in in the cattle trains, whatever we saw on television, at night from the small towns. It was close to [INAUDIBLE], [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE]. From all the small town, they brought them in-- into Sarny. And graves were already prepared. They took them in groups, each town. They took each town separately across the-- the hill, and Germans. When we walked in that day, Germans were sitting right on the hill. That's the camp. If-- this is the answer to the camp that the people were building.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they move everybody from Sarny into this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1942, after the ghetto was, of course-- after the ghetto was surrounded, and-- yes. After the ghetto was surrounded, the next day. The next day, you know, we send out a man, one of our people. We were about-- about-- what did I say? About how many people that were there in this-- in this hiding? So we sent out a man to find out what's going on. When he came back, a Mr. [? Loria ?], a very respected, nice gentleman. He was a-- he was a businessman before the war, and quite a well-to-do man. And he came running back. And he said they are [? appealing-- ?] they-- we must come-- come to the-- to the-- to the-- to the square. If we are not, anybody who they'll find hiding, they will kill him on the spot. We-- we didn't, at that time, we didn't know that the verdict was the same for this one. I decided to-- to-- to-- to meet up with my family, with my-- with my family. And I left them, and I went to-- with my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went into the camp with your family. Describe how you were living in the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was tough. We were, like I described you, in three-- there were three little rooms, 14 people, and the fear, [? everything ?] was horrible. At night, I remember we were not far from the gate of the ghetto. And at night the-- the people, the Christian people of not Jewish persuasion, had homes in the ghetto. So they got-- they were-- they left it-- they left, to leave down there. They didn't have to move out. And so at night, the people who used-- they did-- we had a curfew, 8 o'clock. They didn't have no curfew. So at night, we used to hear some trucks would come in, into the ghetto. So we didn't know if this is a truck that was coming in to-- to stop at the house to drag out people to be killed or whatever it is. But all they were-- some-- the truck belonged to a Christian family who was coming at night around 10:00, 11 o'clock from his work and going back home. And in this fear, that's the way we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when your family moved into the camp, how were you housed? Were there barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- what do you mean, to the camp-- the camp? Was-- was-- we were taken out from the ghetto. You know, we had to come to the square of the ghetto each, the whole-- all the people from Sarny ghetto had to come. And then there was a guy, a German, and I will never forget his-- his face. He was standing with a little table in front of him. And he was alphabetically calling out names, putting them into-- into groups of 30-40, putting them behind the gate, and right away be surrounded by Germans and Ukrainians. And they take them away right away someplace. I, my family name was Szyr. So I was almost at the end to leave the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were not taking you to a place to live? They were executing these people.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us down there to be executed.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: We [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
